Q25. Describe the Box–Muller method for generating normal random variables. How would you generate a sample from N(μ,σ2)N(\mu,\sigma^2)N(μ,σ2)?
Q26. How would you simulate a random sample from the density f(x)=4Γ(2) x e−2xf(x)=\frac{4}{\Gamma(2)}\,x\,e^{-2x}f(x)=Γ(2)4​xe−2x, x≥0x\ge 0x≥0?
Q27. Find the constant α\alphaα so that f(x)=α x(1−x)2f(x)=\alpha\,x(1-x)^2f(x)=αx(1−x)2, 0≤x≤10\le x\le 10≤x≤1, is a density, and describe how to simulate from it.
Q28. Describe the acceptance–rejection method for a continuous distribution. Use it to generate standard normal variates, and give the R code.
Q29. Simulate a random sample from f(x)=2π x1/2e−xf(x)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,x^{1/2}e^{-x}f(x)=π​2​x1/2e−x, x≥0x\ge 0x≥0, using an exponential envelope YYY that has the same mean as XXX.
Q30. Explain Johnk's method for generating Beta(a,b)(a,b)(a,b) random variables. Use R to draw a sample of size 100 from Beta(2,2.5)(2,2.5)(2,2.5).
Q31. Use the acceptance–rejection method with envelope g(x)=1g(x)=1g(x)=1 to simulate from f(x)=20x(1−x)3f(x)=20x(1-x)^3f(x)=20x(1−x)3, 0≤x≤10\le x\le 10≤x≤1. Compute the constant ccc.
Q32. Describe the Marsaglia polar method and compare it with Box–Muller. Use R to draw a sample of size 100 from N(3,4)N(3,4)N(3,4).
Group 6 — Special Distributions & Stochastic Processes (Q34–Q43)
Q34. A discrete random variable XXX has P(X=0,1,2,3,4)=0.25,0.30,0.20,0.15,0.10P(X=0,1,2,3,4)=0.25,0.30,0.20,0.15,0.10P(X=0,1,2,3,4)=0.25,0.30,0.20,0.15,0.10. Describe an efficient method to simulate XXX.
Q35. A continuous variable has density f(x)=x−22f(x)=\frac{x-2}{2}f(x)=2x−2​ for 2≤x≤32\le x\le 32≤x≤3 and f(x)=1−x6f(x)=1-\frac{x}{6}f(x)=1−6x​ for 3≤x≤63\le x\le 63≤x≤6. Estimate its mean and variance from 10,000 simulations.
Q36. Write R code to simulate 10,000 values from the double-exponential density f(x)=e2xf(x)=e^{2x}f(x)=e2x for x<0x<0x<0 and f(x)=e−2xf(x)=e^{-2x}f(x)=e−2x for x≥0x\ge0x≥0. Estimate the mean and variance.
Q37. How would you generate a sample from a multivariate normal distribution using uniform random numbers?
Q38. Find the constant aaa so that f(x)=a x3e−x/2f(x)=a\,x^3 e^{-x/2}f(x)=ax3e−x/2, x≥0x\ge0x≥0, is a density. Give two algorithms to simulate from it and use one to draw 100,000 values.
Q39. Explain Gibbs sampling. Write the algorithm.
Q40. Write an algorithm to simulate the first TTT time units of a Poisson process with rate λ\lambdaλ.
Q41. Buses arrive at a stadium according to a Poisson process at rate 5 per hour. Each bus carries a Uniform(20,40)(20,40)(20,40) number of fans. Simulate the number of fans that have arrived by time t=1t=1t=1 hour.
Q42. A pair of fair coins is tossed until both come up heads. Estimate the average number of tosses by simulation.
Q43. Let N=min⁡{n: U1+U2+⋯+Un>1}N=\min\{n:\ U_1+U_2+\dots+U_n>1\}N=min{n: U1​+U2​+⋯+Un​>1} where Ui∼Uniform(0,1)U_i\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)Ui​∼Uniform(0,1). Estimate E[N]E[N]E[N] using 100, 1000, 10000, 100000100,\ 1000,\ 10000,\ 100000100, 1000, 10000, 100000 simulations. (The true value is eee.)

Group 1 — Simulation Foundations & Monte Carlo Integration (Q1–Q7)

Q1. What is simulation? How does statistical simulation differ from deterministic simulation?

Solution.

Simulation means imitating the behaviour of a real system by building a model of it and then running that model over time (often on a computer) to study how the system behaves. Instead of experimenting on the real system (which may be costly, slow, or impossible), we experiment on the model.

There are two broad kinds of simulation:

  • Deterministic simulation: the model has no randomness. The same inputs always give exactly the same output. It is built only from fixed equations (for example, solving a differential equation for the motion of a planet). There are no random numbers involved.
  • Statistical (stochastic) simulation: the model contains random components. We generate random numbers to imitate uncertainty (for example, customer arrivals, dice rolls, measurement error). Because of this randomness, each run can give a different result, so we run it many times and study the distribution of the outputs (mean, variance, probabilities).

In short: deterministic simulation reproduces one fixed outcome; statistical simulation uses random numbers and produces outcomes that vary, which we then summarise statistically.

Q2. What are the steps of conducting a simulation model?

Solution. A standard simulation study follows these steps in order:

  1. Define the problem and objectives. State clearly what question the simulation must answer.
  1. Formulate the model. Identify the variables, inputs, outputs, and the rules/relationships that connect them.
  1. Collect data and choose input distributions. Decide which probability distributions describe the random inputs, and estimate their parameters.
  1. Build (program) the model. Translate the model into computer code.
  1. Verify and validate. Verify that the code does what you intended; validate that the model behaves like the real system.
  1. Design the experiment. Choose the number of runs nnn, run length, and which scenarios to test.
  1. Run the simulation and collect the output data.
  1. Analyse the output. Compute estimates such as the sample mean, standard error, and confidence intervals.
  1. Document and report the conclusions.

Q3. Explain the principles of the Monte Carlo method. Discuss the simple Monte Carlo method of integration. Approximate I=∫121x dxI=\int_1^2 \frac{1}{x}\,dxI=∫12​x1​dx by simple MC and compare with the exact value.

Solution.

The Monte Carlo method solves a problem by using random sampling. Its core principle is to rewrite the quantity we want (an integral, a sum, a probability) as the expected value of a random variable, and then estimate that expectation by an average over many random draws.

The principles are:

  1. Represent the target as an expectation. Write the unknown quantity as θ=E[h(X)]\theta = E[h(X)]θ=E[h(X)] for some random variable XXX with a known distribution.
  1. Generate random samples. Draw independent values X1,X2,…,XnX_1, X_2, \dots, X_nX1​,X2​,…,Xn​ from that distribution.
  1. Average them. Estimate θ\thetaθ by the sample mean θ^=1n∑i=1nh(Xi)\hat\theta = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} h(X_i)θ^=n1​∑i=1n​h(Xi​).
  1. Rely on the Law of Large Numbers. As n→∞n \to \inftyn→∞, θ^→θ\hat\theta \to \thetaθ^→θ, so the estimate becomes accurate for large samples.
  1. Measure the error. By the Central Limit Theorem the error is about σ/n\sigma/\sqrt{n}σ/n​, so accuracy improves like 1/n1/\sqrt{n}1/n​.

Simple Monte Carlo integration — worked example.

Step 1 — Exact value (for comparison).

Step 2 — Write the integral as an expectation. For a general integral I=∫abg(x) dxI=\int_a^b g(x)\,dxI=∫ab​g(x)dx, multiply and divide by the length (b−a)(b-a)(b−a):

where U∼Uniform(a,b)U\sim \text{Uniform}(a,b)U∼Uniform(a,b), because 1b−a\frac{1}{b-a}b−a1​ is exactly the density of a Uniform(a,b)(a,b)(a,b) random variable.

Step 3 — Apply it here. Here a=1a=1a=1, b=2b=2b=2, so b−a=1b-a=1b−a=1 and g(x)=1xg(x)=\frac1xg(x)=x1​. Therefore

Step 4 — Form the estimator. Draw U1,…,Un∼Uniform(1,2)U_1,\dots,U_n \sim \text{Uniform}(1,2)U1​,…,Un​∼Uniform(1,2) and average:

Step 5 — Why it works. By the Law of Large Numbers I^→E[1/U]=I\hat I \to E[1/U] = II^→E[1/U]=I as nnn grows. For large nnn the simulated value will be very close to ln⁡2≈0.6931\ln 2 \approx 0.6931ln2≈0.6931.

Algorithm.

  1. Choose a large sample size nnn.
  1. Generate U1,…,UnU_1,\dots,U_nU1​,…,Un​ from Uniform(1,2)(1,2)(1,2).
  1. Compute g(Ui)=1/Uig(U_i)=1/U_ig(Ui​)=1/Ui​ for each iii.
  1. Average them: I^=1n∑1/Ui\hat I = \frac1n\sum 1/U_iI^=n1​∑1/Ui​.
  1. Report I^\hat II^ and compare with ln⁡2\ln 2ln2.

R code.

# Simple Monte Carlo estimate of the integral of 1/x from 1 to 2
set.seed(123)                # makes the result reproducible
n  <- 1e6                    # number of random points
U  <- runif(n, min = 1, max = 2)   # U ~ Uniform(1,2)
I_hat <- mean(1 / U)         # (b-a)=1, so estimate is just the mean of 1/U

I_hat                        # Monte Carlo estimate
log(2)                       # exact value for comparison

Expected output: I_hat is approximately 0.6931, essentially equal to the exact value log(2) = 0.6931472. The tiny difference is Monte Carlo error and shrinks as nnn increases.

Q4. What is Monte Carlo integration? Describe any two variance-reduction techniques.

Solution.

Monte Carlo integration estimates an integral by interpreting it as an expectation and averaging random samples, as in Q3. The plain ("crude") estimator I^=1n∑g(Ui)\hat I = \frac1n\sum g(U_i)I^=n1​∑g(Ui​) is unbiased but its error depends on the variance of g(U)g(U)g(U). Variance-reduction techniques redesign the estimator so it has the same mean but a smaller variance, giving a more accurate answer for the same nnn. Two common techniques:

(a) Antithetic variates. Instead of using independent uniforms, pair each UUU with its mirror 1−U1-U1−U. If U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) then 1−U1-U1−U is also Uniform(0,1)(0,1)(0,1), and for a monotone ggg the two are negatively correlated. The estimator

has variance 12(Var(g(U))+Cov(g(U),g(1−U)))\tfrac12\big(\text{Var}(g(U)) + \text{Cov}(g(U),g(1-U))\big)21​(Var(g(U))+Cov(g(U),g(1−U))). The negative covariance lowers the variance.

(b) Control variates. Use a second function fff whose mean μf=E[f(U)]\mu_f=E[f(U)]μf​=E[f(U)] is known exactly and which is correlated with ggg. Define

This is unbiased for any constant ccc because E[f(U)−μf]=0E[f(U)-\mu_f]=0E[f(U)−μf​]=0. The variance is minimised by

which can cut the variance substantially when ggg and fff are strongly correlated.

(Other techniques include importance sampling and stratified sampling, covered later.)

Q5. Explain control-variates Monte Carlo simulation for integration. Write the algorithm and estimate I=∫0111+x dxI=\int_0^1 \frac{1}{1+x}\,dxI=∫01​1+x1​dx using control variates.

Solution.

Step 1 — Exact value (for comparison).

Step 2 — Set up the crude estimator. With U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) and g(x)=11+xg(x)=\frac{1}{1+x}g(x)=1+x1​,

Step 3 — Choose a control variate. We need a function correlated with g(x)=11+xg(x)=\frac{1}{1+x}g(x)=1+x1​ whose mean is known. A natural choice is the linear function f(x)=1+xf(x)=1+xf(x)=1+x, because 11+x\frac{1}{1+x}1+x1​ decreases as 1+x1+x1+x increases, so they are strongly (negatively) correlated. Its known mean is

Step 4 — Control-variate estimator.

In practice we estimate c∗c^{*}c∗ from the simulated sample using the sample covariance and variance.

Step 5 — Result. Both the crude mean and the control-variate estimate are close to ln⁡2≈0.6931\ln 2 \approx 0.6931ln2≈0.6931, but the control-variate version has noticeably smaller variance.

Algorithm.

  1. Generate U1,…,Un∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,\dots,U_n \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,…,Un​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Compute gi=1/(1+Ui)g_i = 1/(1+U_i)gi​=1/(1+Ui​) and fi=1+Uif_i = 1+U_ifi​=1+Ui​.
  1. Estimate c∗=−Cov^(g,f)/Var^(f)c^{*} = -\widehat{\text{Cov}}(g,f)/\widehat{\text{Var}}(f)c∗=−Cov(g,f)/Var(f) from the sample.
  1. Form the adjusted values Yi=gi+c∗(fi−3/2)Y_i = g_i + c^{*}(f_i - 3/2)Yi​=gi​+c∗(fi​−3/2).
  1. Estimate I^c=Yˉ\hat I_c = \bar YI^c​=Yˉ and compare its variance with that of gˉ\bar ggˉ​.

R code.

# Control-variate Monte Carlo for integral of 1/(1+x) on [0,1]
set.seed(123)
n  <- 1e5
U  <- runif(n)               # U ~ Uniform(0,1)

g  <- 1 / (1 + U)            # target function
f  <- 1 + U                  # control variate, known mean = 3/2
mu_f <- 3/2                  # E[1+U]

# Optimal coefficient c* estimated from the sample
c_star <- -cov(g, f) / var(f)

Y      <- g + c_star * (f - mu_f)   # adjusted estimator
I_crude <- mean(g)                  # plain Monte Carlo
I_cv    <- mean(Y)                  # control-variate estimate

I_crude; I_cv; log(2)               # compare with exact value
var(g)/n; var(Y)/n                  # variance of each estimator (Y is smaller)

Expected output: both I_crude and I_cv are near 0.6931, matching log(2). The variance var(Y) is much smaller than var(g), so the control-variate estimate is more precise for the same nnn.

Q6. Use control variates to estimate θ=∫01e−x1+x2 dx\theta=\int_0^1 \frac{e^{-x}}{1+x^2}\,dxθ=∫01​1+x2e−x​dx, where θ=E[g(X)]\theta=E[g(X)]θ=E[g(X)] for X∼U(0,1)X\sim U(0,1)X∼U(0,1) and g(x)=e−x1+x2g(x)=\frac{e^{-x}}{1+x^2}g(x)=1+x2e−x​. (a) Use f1(x)=11+x2f_1(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^2}f1​(x)=1+x21​; (b) use f2(x)=x1+x2f_2(x)=\frac{x}{1+x^2}f2​(x)=1+x2x​; (c) compare both with simple Monte Carlo — which gives greater variance reduction?

Solution.

Step 1 — The target. With X∼Uniform(0,1)X\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)X∼Uniform(0,1),

The crude estimator is θ^=1n∑g(Ui)\hat\theta = \frac1n\sum g(U_i)θ^=n1​∑g(Ui​).

Step 2 — Known means of the control variates. We must know E[f1]E[f_1]E[f1​] and E[f2]E[f_2]E[f2​] exactly.

Step 3 — Control-variate estimators. For each choice fkf_kfk​ (with mean μk\mu_kμk​):

Step 4 — Why f1f_1f1​ should win. The factor 11+x2\frac{1}{1+x^2}1+x21​ appears inside g(x)=e−x⋅11+x2g(x)=e^{-x}\cdot\frac{1}{1+x^2}g(x)=e−x⋅1+x21​, and e−xe^{-x}e−x is close to a smooth, mild factor on [0,1][0,1][0,1]. So ggg behaves almost like a constant multiple of f1f_1f1​, making ggg and f1f_1f1​ very strongly correlated. The function f2(x)=x1+x2f_2(x)=\frac{x}{1+x^2}f2​(x)=1+x2x​ rises from 0, so it tracks ggg less well. Therefore f1f_1f1​ removes more variance.

Step 5 — Conclusion. All three estimators give θ≈0.5248\theta\approx 0.5248θ≈0.5248. Both control variates beat simple Monte Carlo, and f1f_1f1​ achieves the greater variance reduction because it is more highly correlated with ggg.

Algorithm.

  1. Generate U1,…,Un∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,\dots,U_n\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,…,Un​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Compute gi=e−Ui/(1+Ui2)g_i=e^{-U_i}/(1+U_i^2)gi​=e−Ui​/(1+Ui2​).
  1. For each control variate fkf_kfk​: compute fk,if_{k,i}fk,i​, estimate ck∗=−Cov^(g,fk)/Var^(fk)c_k^{*}=-\widehat{\text{Cov}}(g,f_k)/\widehat{\text{Var}}(f_k)ck∗​=−Cov(g,fk​)/Var(fk​).
  1. Form Yk,i=gi+ck∗(fk,i−μk)Y_{k,i}=g_i+c_k^{*}(f_{k,i}-\mu_k)Yk,i​=gi​+ck∗​(fk,i​−μk​) and average.
  1. Compare Var(gˉ)\text{Var}(\bar g)Var(gˉ​), Var(Yˉ1)\text{Var}(\bar Y_1)Var(Yˉ1​), Var(Yˉ2)\text{Var}(\bar Y_2)Var(Yˉ2​).

R code.

# Control variates for theta = integral of e^{-x}/(1+x^2) on [0,1]
set.seed(2024)
n  <- 1e5
U  <- runif(n)

g  <- exp(-U) / (1 + U^2)        # target g(x)

f1 <- 1 / (1 + U^2)             # control variate 1, mean = pi/4
f2 <- U / (1 + U^2)             # control variate 2, mean = (1/2)ln2
mu1 <- pi/4
mu2 <- 0.5 * log(2)

# Optimal coefficients estimated from the sample
c1 <- -cov(g, f1) / var(f1)
c2 <- -cov(g, f2) / var(f2)

Y1 <- g + c1 * (f1 - mu1)
Y2 <- g + c2 * (f2 - mu2)

mean(g); mean(Y1); mean(Y2)     # all near 0.5248

# Variance reduction: compare variances of the three estimators
var(g); var(Y1); var(Y2)
cat("Reduction with f1:", 1 - var(Y1)/var(g), "\n")
cat("Reduction with f2:", 1 - var(Y2)/var(g), "\n")

Expected output: all three means are about 0.5248. The variance with f1 is the smallest — typically a reduction of well over 95%, much larger than the reduction from f2. So f1f_1f1​ gives greater variance reduction.

Q7. Explain control variate and regression for MC integration. Approximate the integral of Q5, I=∫0111+x dxI=\int_0^1 \frac{1}{1+x}\,dxI=∫01​1+x1​dx, using the control-variate and regression MC method.

Solution.

Step 1 — The link between control variates and regression. Recall the control-variate estimator uses Yi=g(Ui)+c (f(Ui)−μf)Y_i = g(U_i) + c\,(f(U_i)-\mu_f)Yi​=g(Ui​)+c(f(Ui​)−μf​) with optimal coefficient

Now compare this with simple linear regression of ggg on fff. If we fit the line g≈β0+β1fg \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 fg≈β0​+β1​f by least squares, the slope is

So the best control-variate coefficient is just the negative of the regression slope of ggg on fff. This is why control variates is often called the regression method: we are removing the part of ggg that is linearly predictable from fff (whose mean we know), leaving a lower-variance remainder.

Step 2 — The regression estimator of the integral. Center the control variate and regress:

Taking expectations, E[g]=β0E[g] = \beta_0E[g]=β0​ because E[f−μf]=0E[f-\mu_f]=0E[f−μf​]=0. Hence the least-squares intercept β^0\hat\beta_0β^​0​ is exactly the estimate of the integral III, and it has smaller variance than the raw mean gˉ\bar ggˉ​.

Step 3 — Apply to I=∫0111+x dx=ln⁡2I=\int_0^1\frac{1}{1+x}\,dx=\ln 2I=∫01​1+x1​dx=ln2. Use the same control variate as in Q5, f(x)=1+xf(x)=1+xf(x)=1+x with μf=32\mu_f = \tfrac32μf​=23​. Regress gi=1/(1+Ui)g_i=1/(1+U_i)gi​=1/(1+Ui​) on the centred (fi−32)(f_i-\tfrac32)(fi​−23​); the intercept estimates ln⁡2≈0.6931\ln 2\approx 0.6931ln2≈0.6931.

Algorithm.

  1. Generate U1,…,Un∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,\dots,U_n\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,…,Un​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Compute gi=1/(1+Ui)g_i=1/(1+U_i)gi​=1/(1+Ui​) and centred control variate xi=fi−μf=(1+Ui)−32x_i=f_i-\mu_f=(1+U_i)-\tfrac32xi​=fi​−μf​=(1+Ui​)−23​.
  1. Fit the least-squares regression gi=β0+β1xi+εig_i = \beta_0+\beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_igi​=β0​+β1​xi​+εi​.
  1. Report the intercept β^0\hat\beta_0β^​0​ as the integral estimate (equivalently gˉ+c∗(fˉ−μf)\bar g + c^{*}(\bar f-\mu_f)gˉ​+c∗(fˉ​−μf​) with c∗=−β^1c^{*}=-\hat\beta_1c∗=−β^​1​).
  1. Compare with ln⁡2\ln 2ln2.

R code.

# Control variate via regression for integral of 1/(1+x) on [0,1]
set.seed(7)
n   <- 1e5
U   <- runif(n)

g   <- 1 / (1 + U)           # target
f   <- 1 + U                 # control variate (known mean 3/2)
x   <- f - 3/2               # centred control variate

fit <- lm(g ~ x)            # least-squares regression of g on centred f
beta0 <- coef(fit)[1]        # intercept = estimate of the integral

beta0                        # ~ 0.6931
log(2)                       # exact value

# Equivalent control-variate form
c_star <- -coef(fit)[2]      # c* = -slope
mean(g) + c_star * (mean(f) - 3/2)

Expected output: the intercept beta0 is about 0.6931, matching log(2), and the control-variate form gives the same number. The regression residual variance is far smaller than var(g), confirming the variance reduction.

Group 2 — Variance Reduction & Random Number Generation (Q8–Q14)

Q8. What do you mean by antithetic variates? What are the advantages of antithetic variates in MC integration? Discuss how these variates can be used as control variates.

Solution.

Step 1 — Definition. Antithetic variates are pairs of random numbers that are negatively correlated on purpose. The simplest pair is UUU and 1−U1-U1−U: if U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) then 1−U1-U1−U is also Uniform(0,1)(0,1)(0,1), but when UUU is large 1−U1-U1−U is small.

Step 2 — The estimator. To estimate θ=E[g(U)]\theta=E[g(U)]θ=E[g(U)], use the average of each value and its antithetic partner:

Its variance is

When ggg is monotone, g(U)g(U)g(U) and g(1−U)g(1-U)g(1−U) are negatively correlated, so the covariance term is negative and the variance drops below that of crude MC.

Step 3 — Advantages.

  1. It reduces variance whenever ggg is monotone (very common).
  1. It needs fewer uniform draws (each UUU does double duty through 1−U1-U1−U).
  1. It is simple to program and adds almost no extra cost.

Step 4 — Link to control variates. The antithetic pair can be read as a control-variate idea: the term g(1−U)g(1-U)g(1−U) has the same known mean θ\thetaθ as g(U)g(U)g(U), so g(1−U)−θg(1-U)-\thetag(1−U)−θ acts like a centred control variate with coefficient c=1c=1c=1. Adding it cancels part of the random fluctuation in g(U)g(U)g(U), exactly as a control variate does.

Algorithm.

  1. Generate U1,…,Un∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,\dots,U_n\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,…,Un​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. For each iii form the pair UiU_iUi​ and 1−Ui1-U_i1−Ui​.
  1. Compute 12(g(Ui)+g(1−Ui))\tfrac12\big(g(U_i)+g(1-U_i)\big)21​(g(Ui​)+g(1−Ui​)).
  1. Average these nnn values to get θ^a\hat\theta_aθ^a​.

R code.

# Antithetic variates for theta = integral of e^x on [0,1] (true value e - 1)
set.seed(1)
n <- 1e5
U <- runif(n)

g <- function(x) exp(x)

# Crude estimator (2n points) vs antithetic estimator (n pairs)
crude      <- mean(g(runif(2*n)))
antithetic <- mean((g(U) + g(1 - U)) / 2)

crude; antithetic; exp(1) - 1          # all near 1.7183
var((g(U) + g(1 - U)) / 2); var(g(U))  # antithetic variance is much smaller

Expected output: both estimates are near 1.7183 (e−1e-1e−1), but the antithetic estimator has a far smaller variance than the crude one.

Q9. How do you use stratified sampling for MC simulations? Using stratified sampling, approximate θ=∫1411+x dx\theta=\int_1^4 \frac{1}{1+\sqrt{x}}\,dxθ=∫14​1+x​1​dx. Compare with simple MC and the exact value.

Solution.

Step 1 — Exact value (for comparison). Substitute u=xu=\sqrt{x}u=x​, so x=u2x=u^2x=u2, dx=2u dudx=2u\,dudx=2udu; limits x:1→4x:1\to4x:1→4 become u:1→2u:1\to2u:1→2.

Step 2 — Idea of stratified sampling. Split the integration range [1,4][1,4][1,4] into kkk non-overlapping strata of equal width. Draw the same number of points from each stratum instead of letting all points fall randomly. Because each stratum is sampled for sure, the estimator avoids the extra randomness of “how many points landed where,” which lowers variance.

Step 3 — Estimator. Write θ=∫14g(x) dx\theta=\int_1^4 g(x)\,dxθ=∫14​g(x)dx with g(x)=11+xg(x)=\frac{1}{1+\sqrt{x}}g(x)=1+x​1​. Divide [1,4][1,4][1,4] into kkk strata [aj−1,aj][a_{j-1},a_j][aj−1​,aj​] each of width h=3kh=\frac{3}{k}h=k3​. In stratum jjj draw mmm uniforms Vj,1,…,Vj,m∼Uniform(aj−1,aj)V_{j,1},\dots,V_{j,m}\sim\text{Uniform}(a_{j-1},a_j)Vj,1​,…,Vj,m​∼Uniform(aj−1​,aj​). Then

Step 4 — Simple MC (for comparison). With U∼Uniform(1,4)U\sim\text{Uniform}(1,4)U∼Uniform(1,4) and length b−a=3b-a=3b−a=3, θ^MC=3⋅1N∑g(Ui)\hat\theta_{MC}=3\cdot\frac1N\sum g(U_i)θ^MC​=3⋅N1​∑g(Ui​).

Step 5 — Result. Both estimates are close to 1.189071.189071.18907, but the stratified estimate has a smaller standard error.

Algorithm.

  1. Choose strata count kkk and points per stratum mmm (total N=kmN=kmN=km), width h=3/kh=3/kh=3/k.
  1. For each stratum j=1,…,kj=1,\dots,kj=1,…,k: draw mmm uniforms in [1+(j−1)h, 1+jh][1+(j-1)h,\,1+jh][1+(j−1)h,1+jh].
  1. Compute the stratum average of ggg and multiply by hhh.
  1. Sum over strata to get θ^strat\hat\theta_{strat}θ^strat​.
  1. Compare with simple MC and with 1.189071.189071.18907.

R code.

# Stratified sampling for integral of 1/(1+sqrt(x)) on [1,4]
set.seed(10)
g <- function(x) 1 / (1 + sqrt(x))

# ---- Simple Monte Carlo ----
N <- 1e5
U <- runif(N, 1, 4)
I_mc <- 3 * mean(g(U))            # (b-a)=3

# ---- Stratified sampling ----
k <- 100                          # number of strata
m <- N / k                        # points per stratum
h <- 3 / k                        # stratum width
edges <- seq(1, 4, by = h)        # stratum boundaries

I_strat <- 0
for (j in 1:k) {
  V <- runif(m, edges[j], edges[j+1])   # sample inside stratum j
  I_strat <- I_strat + h * mean(g(V))   # add stratum contribution
}

exact <- 2 - 2*log(1.5)
I_mc; I_strat; exact              # all near 1.18907

Expected output: I_mc and I_strat are both near 1.18907 (the exact value), and repeating the experiment shows I_strat varies much less than I_mc.

Q10. How would you generate a random permutation? Write down the steps.

Solution.

To get a permutation of {1,2,…,n}\{1,2,\dots,n\}{1,2,…,n} in which every ordering is equally likely, use the Fisher–Yates (Knuth) shuffle. The idea: walk from the last position to the first, and at each position swap the current element with a randomly chosen earlier-or-equal element.

Why it is uniform: at the first swap, any of the nnn items is equally likely to land in the last slot; at the next swap any of the remaining n−1n-1n−1 is equally likely for the next slot, and so on, giving each of the n!n!n! orderings probability 1/n!1/n!1/n!.

Algorithm.

  1. Start with the array a=(1,2,…,n)a=(1,2,\dots,n)a=(1,2,…,n).
  1. For i=n,n−1,…,2i=n, n-1, \dots, 2i=n,n−1,…,2:
    1. Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) and set j=⌈iU⌉j=\lceil iU\rceilj=⌈iU⌉ (a random index in {1,…,i}\{1,\dots,i\}{1,…,i}).
    1. Swap a[i]a[i]a[i] and a[j]a[j]a[j].
  1. The final array aaa is the random permutation.

R code.

# Random permutation of 1..n by the Fisher-Yates shuffle
random_perm <- function(n) {
  a <- 1:n
  for (i in n:2) {
    j <- sample.int(i, 1)   # uniform index in 1..i
    tmp <- a[i]; a[i] <- a[j]; a[j] <- tmp   # swap
  }
  a
}

set.seed(5)
random_perm(10)             # one random ordering of 1..10

Expected output: a shuffled vector containing each of 1..10 exactly once, e.g. 3 7 1 10 5 ...; a different order appears for each seed.

Q11. How would you generate pseudo-random numbers using the linear congruential method? Explain with an example.

Solution.

Step 1 — The recurrence. The linear congruential generator (LCG) produces integers by

where aaa is the multiplier, ccc the increment, mmm the modulus, and X0X_0X0​ the seed. To get numbers in [0,1)[0,1)[0,1) we divide:

Step 2 — Example. Take a=5a=5a=5, c=3c=3c=3, m=16m=16m=16, seed X0=7X_0=7X0​=7.

So the first three pseudo-random numbers are 0.3750, 0.0625, 0.50000.3750,\ 0.0625,\ 0.50000.3750, 0.0625, 0.5000. The sequence eventually repeats with a period at most mmm; good choices of a,c,ma,c,ma,c,m make the period long.

Algorithm.

  1. Choose a,c,ma,c,ma,c,m and seed X0X_0X0​.
  1. For n=0,1,2,…n=0,1,2,\dotsn=0,1,2,… compute Xn+1=(aXn+c) mod mX_{n+1}=(aX_n+c)\bmod mXn+1​=(aXn​+c)modm.
  1. Output Un+1=Xn+1/mU_{n+1}=X_{n+1}/mUn+1​=Xn+1​/m.
  1. Stop when enough numbers are produced.

R code.

# Linear congruential generator
lcg <- function(n, a, c, m, seed) {
  x <- numeric(n)
  xi <- seed
  for (i in 1:n) {
    xi <- (a * xi + c) %% m   # recurrence
    x[i] <- xi
  }
  x / m                      # scale to [0,1)
}

lcg(3, a = 5, c = 3, m = 16, seed = 7)   # 0.3750 0.0625 0.5000

Expected output: 0.3750 0.0625 0.5000, matching the hand calculation.

Q12. What are the additive congruential and multiplicative congruential methods? Explain them with examples.

Solution.

Both are special cases of the linear congruential recurrence Xn+1=(aXn+c) mod mX_{n+1}=(aX_n+c)\bmod mXn+1​=(aXn​+c)modm.

(a) Additive congruential method (a=1a=1a=1). The recurrence becomes

Example: c=3c=3c=3, m=16m=16m=16, X0=7X_0=7X0​=7: X1=(7+3) mod 16=10X_1=(7+3)\bmod16=10X1​=(7+3)mod16=10, X2=(10+3) mod 16=13X_2=(10+3)\bmod16=13X2​=(10+3)mod16=13, X3=(13+3) mod 16=0X_3=(13+3)\bmod16=0X3​=(13+3)mod16=0. So UUU values are 10/16,13/16,010/16,13/16,010/16,13/16,0. (A purely additive generator is weak — it just steps by a constant — so it is rarely used alone; a stronger variant is the lagged-Fibonacci form Xn+1=(Xn+Xn−k) mod mX_{n+1}=(X_n+X_{n-k})\bmod mXn+1​=(Xn​+Xn−k​)modm.)

(b) Multiplicative congruential method (c=0c=0c=0). The recurrence becomes

Example: a=5a=5a=5, m=16m=16m=16, X0=7X_0=7X0​=7: X1=(5⋅7) mod 16=35 mod 16=3X_1=(5\cdot7)\bmod16=35\bmod16=3X1​=(5⋅7)mod16=35mod16=3, X2=(5⋅3) mod 16=15X_2=(5\cdot3)\bmod16=15X2​=(5⋅3)mod16=15, X3=(5⋅15) mod 16=75 mod 16=11X_3=(5\cdot15)\bmod16=75\bmod16=11X3​=(5⋅15)mod16=75mod16=11. So UUU values are 3/16,15/16,11/163/16,15/16,11/163/16,15/16,11/16. The seed must be non-zero, and good a,ma,ma,m (often mmm a large prime or power of 2) give a long period.

R code.

# Additive (a = 1) and multiplicative (c = 0) congruential generators
add_cong  <- function(n, c, m, seed) {
  x <- numeric(n); xi <- seed
  for (i in 1:n) { xi <- (xi + c) %% m; x[i] <- xi }
  x / m
}
mult_cong <- function(n, a, m, seed) {
  x <- numeric(n); xi <- seed
  for (i in 1:n) { xi <- (a * xi) %% m; x[i] <- xi }
  x / m
}

add_cong(3, c = 3, m = 16, seed = 7)    # 0.6250 0.8125 0.0000
mult_cong(3, a = 5, m = 16, seed = 7)   # 0.1875 0.9375 0.6875

Expected output: the additive generator returns 0.6250 0.8125 0.0000 and the multiplicative one returns 0.1875 0.9375 0.6875, matching the hand calculations.

Q13. Using the linear congruential method, generate the first 3 random numbers with seed 298529852985 and parameters a=32, c=3, m=5821a=32,\ c=3,\ m=5821a=32, c=3, m=5821. Report the random numbers to 5 decimal places.

Solution.

Use Xn+1=(32Xn+3) mod 5821X_{n+1}=(32X_n+3)\bmod 5821Xn+1​=(32Xn​+3)mod5821 with X0=2985X_0=2985X0​=2985, then U=X/5821U=X/5821U=X/5821.

Step 1 — First number.

Divide by 582158215821: 5821×16=931365821\times16=931365821×16=93136, remainder 95523−93136=238795523-93136=238795523−93136=2387. So

Step 2 — Second number.

5821×13=756735821\times13=756735821×13=75673, remainder 76387−75673=71476387-75673=71476387−75673=714. So

Step 3 — Third number.

5821×3=174635821\times3=174635821×3=17463, remainder 22851−17463=538822851-17463=538822851−17463=5388. So

Answer: U1=0.41007, U2=0.12266, U3=0.92561U_1=0.41007,\ U_2=0.12266,\ U_3=0.92561U1​=0.41007, U2​=0.12266, U3​=0.92561.

R code.

# LCG: seed 2985, a = 32, c = 3, m = 5821, first 3 numbers (5 dp)
lcg <- function(n, a, c, m, seed) {
  x <- numeric(n); xi <- seed
  for (i in 1:n) { xi <- (a*xi + c) %% m; x[i] <- xi }
  x / m
}
round(lcg(3, a = 32, c = 3, m = 5821, seed = 2985), 5)

Expected output: 0.41007 0.12266 0.92561, matching the hand calculation.

Q14. Using the multiplicative congruence method, generate the first 10 random numbers with seed 693153693153693153 and parameters a=73a=7^3a=73 and m=217−1m=2^{17}-1m=217−1.

Solution.

Here a=73=343a=7^3=343a=73=343 and m=217−1=131071m=2^{17}-1=131071m=217−1=131071, with recurrence Xn+1=(343 Xn) mod 131071X_{n+1}=(343\,X_n)\bmod 131071Xn+1​=(343Xn​)mod131071 and U=X/mU=X/mU=X/m.

Step 1 — Reduce the seed mod mmm. Since 693153>131071693153>131071693153>131071, first reduce: 131071×5=655355131071\times5=655355131071×5=655355, so

Step 2 — First number.

because 131071×98=12,844,958131071\times98=12{,}844{,}958131071×98=12,844,958 and 12,964,714−12,844,958=11975612{,}964{,}714-12{,}844{,}958=11975612,964,714−12,844,958=119756. So U1=119756/131071=0.91367U_1=119756/131071=0.91367U1​=119756/131071=0.91367.

Step 3 — Second number.

since 131071×313=41,025,223131071\times313=41{,}025{,}223131071×313=41,025,223. So U2=51085/131071=0.38975U_2=51085/131071=0.38975U2​=51085/131071=0.38975.

Continuing the same recurrence eight more times gives the full list of 10. Because the arithmetic grows large, it is easiest to let R finish the sequence.

Algorithm.

  1. Set a=343a=343a=343, m=131071m=131071m=131071, X0=693153 mod mX_0=693153\bmod mX0​=693153modm.
  1. For i=1,…,10i=1,\dots,10i=1,…,10: Xi=(aXi−1) mod mX_i=(aX_{i-1})\bmod mXi​=(aXi−1​)modm, Ui=Xi/mU_i=X_i/mUi​=Xi​/m.
  1. Output U1,…,U10U_1,\dots,U_{10}U1​,…,U10​.

R code.

# Multiplicative congruential generator: a = 7^3, m = 2^17 - 1, seed = 693153
mult_cong <- function(n, a, m, seed) {
  x <- numeric(n)
  xi <- seed %% m            # reduce the seed first
  for (i in 1:n) { xi <- (a * xi) %% m; x[i] <- xi }
  x / m
}
round(mult_cong(10, a = 7^3, m = 2^17 - 1, seed = 693153), 5)

Expected output: a vector of 10 numbers in [0,1)[0,1)[0,1) beginning 0.91367 0.38975 ...; the first two match the hand calculation and the rest follow from the same recurrence.

Group 3 — Monte Carlo Estimation Problems (Q15–Q19)

Q15. Let f(x)=41+x2f(x)=\frac{4}{1+x^2}f(x)=1+x24​. Use simple Monte Carlo integration to compute I(f)=∫0141+x2 dxI(f)=\int_0^1 \frac{4}{1+x^2}\,dxI(f)=∫01​1+x24​dx.

Solution.

Step 1 — Exact value (for comparison).

(This is a classic way to estimate π\piπ.)

Step 2 — Write as an expectation. With U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) and length b−a=1b-a=1b−a=1,

Step 3 — Estimator. Draw U1,…,UnU_1,\dots,U_nU1​,…,Un​ and average:

Step 4 — Result. By the Law of Large Numbers I^→π\hat I \to \piI^→π. For large nnn the estimate is close to 3.14163.14163.1416.

Algorithm.

  1. Generate U1,…,Un∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,\dots,U_n\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,…,Un​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Compute 4/(1+Ui2)4/(1+U_i^2)4/(1+Ui2​) for each iii.
  1. Average to get I^\hat II^.
  1. Compare with π\piπ.

R code.

# Simple Monte Carlo for integral of 4/(1+x^2) on [0,1] (= pi)
set.seed(42)
n <- 1e6
U <- runif(n)
I_hat <- mean(4 / (1 + U^2))

I_hat        # ~ 3.1416
pi           # exact value

Expected output: I_hat is approximately 3.1416, matching pi.

Q16. Write a program that implements the “hit-or-miss” Monte Carlo integration algorithm. Find the estimate of I=∫014(1−x2) dxI=\int_0^1 4(1-x^2)\,dxI=∫01​4(1−x2)dx.

Solution.

Step 1 — Exact value (for comparison).

Step 2 — Idea of hit-or-miss. Put the curve y=g(x)=4(1−x2)y=g(x)=4(1-x^2)y=g(x)=4(1−x2) inside a rectangle. On [0,1][0,1][0,1], ggg is largest at x=0x=0x=0 where g(0)=4g(0)=4g(0)=4, so a rectangle [0,1]×[0,4][0,1]\times[0,4][0,1]×[0,4] encloses the whole area under the curve. Throw random points into this rectangle and count the fraction that land under the curve. That fraction estimates the ratio (area under curve)/(area of rectangle).

Step 3 — Estimator. Let the rectangle have area A=(1−0)×4=4A=(1-0)\times 4 = 4A=(1−0)×4=4. Generate points (Xi,Yi)(X_i,Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​) with Xi∼Uniform(0,1)X_i\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)Xi​∼Uniform(0,1) and Yi∼Uniform(0,4)Y_i\sim\text{Uniform}(0,4)Yi​∼Uniform(0,4). A point is a hit if Yi≤g(Xi)Y_i\le g(X_i)Yi​≤g(Xi​). With p^\hat pp^​ = fraction of hits,

Step 4 — Result. p^\hat pp^​ converges to I/A=(8/3)/4=2/3I/A = (8/3)/4 = 2/3I/A=(8/3)/4=2/3, so I^→8/3≈2.6667\hat I \to 8/3\approx 2.6667I^→8/3≈2.6667.

Algorithm.

  1. Set the rectangle height c=4c=4c=4 (the max of ggg on [0,1][0,1][0,1]) and area A=4A=4A=4.
  1. For i=1,…,ni=1,\dots,ni=1,…,n: draw Xi∼Uniform(0,1)X_i\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)Xi​∼Uniform(0,1) and Yi∼Uniform(0,c)Y_i\sim\text{Uniform}(0,c)Yi​∼Uniform(0,c).
  1. Count a hit if Yi≤4(1−Xi2)Y_i\le 4(1-X_i^2)Yi​≤4(1−Xi2​).
  1. Estimate p^=hits/n\hat p=\text{hits}/np^​=hits/n and I^=Ap^\hat I = A\hat pI^=Ap^​.

R code.

# Hit-or-miss Monte Carlo for integral of 4(1 - x^2) on [0,1]
set.seed(99)
n <- 1e6
c <- 4                       # height of the bounding box (max of g on [0,1])
A <- 1 * c                   # rectangle area

X <- runif(n, 0, 1)
Y <- runif(n, 0, c)
hits <- Y <= 4 * (1 - X^2)    # TRUE when the point is under the curve

I_hat <- A * mean(hits)       # area * fraction of hits
I_hat        # ~ 2.6667
8/3          # exact value

Expected output: I_hat is approximately 2.6667, matching 8/3.

Q17. Approximate I=∫01cos⁡x esin⁡x dxI=\int_0^1 \cos x\,e^{\sin x}\,dxI=∫01​cosxesinxdx by MC sampling with n=10,100,500,103,104,105,106,107n=10,100,500,10^3,10^4,10^5,10^6,10^7n=10,100,500,103,104,105,106,107. For each experiment, compute the sample mean, standard error, and relative error.

Solution.

Step 1 — Exact value (for comparison). Notice ddxesin⁡x=cos⁡x esin⁡x\frac{d}{dx}e^{\sin x}=\cos x\,e^{\sin x}dxd​esinx=cosxesinx, so the integrand is an exact derivative:

Step 2 — Estimator and error formulas. With U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) and h(x)=cos⁡x esin⁡xh(x)=\cos x\,e^{\sin x}h(x)=cosxesinx, we have I=E[h(U)]I=E[h(U)]I=E[h(U)]. The estimates for a sample of size nnn are

where sss is the sample standard deviation of the h(Ui)h(U_i)h(Ui​) values.

Step 3 — What to expect. All estimates hover around 1.31981.31981.3198. The standard error shrinks like 1/n1/\sqrt{n}1/n​: each time nnn grows by a factor of 100, the standard error falls by a factor of 10. So at n=10n=10n=10 the relative error may be several percent, but by n=106n=10^6n=106–10710^7107 it is tiny.

Algorithm.

  1. For each sample size nnn in the list:
    1. Generate U1,…,Un∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,\dots,U_n\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,…,Un​∼Uniform(0,1).
    1. Compute hi=cos⁡Ui esin⁡Uih_i=\cos U_i\,e^{\sin U_i}hi​=cosUi​esinUi​.
    1. Compute I^=hˉ\hat I=\bar hI^=hˉ, s=sd(h)s=\text{sd}(h)s=sd(h), SE^=s/n\widehat{SE}=s/\sqrt nSE=s/n​, relative error =SE^/I^=\widehat{SE}/\hat I=SE/I^.
  1. Tabulate the results against the exact value 1.319781.319781.31978.

R code.

# MC integration of cos(x) e^{sin(x)} on [0,1] at several sample sizes
set.seed(2025)
h  <- function(x) cos(x) * exp(sin(x))
ns <- c(10, 100, 500, 1e3, 1e4, 1e5, 1e6, 1e7)

result <- data.frame()
for (n in ns) {
  U   <- runif(n)
  hv  <- h(U)
  est <- mean(hv)              # sample mean (the estimate)
  se  <- sd(hv) / sqrt(n)      # standard error
  rel <- se / est              # relative error
  result <- rbind(result, data.frame(n = n, estimate = est,
                                     std_error = se, rel_error = rel))
}
print(result)
exp(sin(1)) - 1                 # exact value ~ 1.31978

Expected output: a table whose estimate column stays near 1.3198, while std_error and rel_error fall steadily — roughly tenfold for every hundredfold increase in n — confirming the 1/n1/\sqrt{n}1/n​ convergence.

Q18. Use a stratified random sample to integrate I=∫01eu−1e−1 duI=\int_0^1 \frac{e^{u}-1}{e-1}\,duI=∫01​e−1eu−1​du. What do you recommend for the number of intervals (2 or 3) and sample sizes? What is the efficiency gain over simple Monte Carlo?

Solution.

Step 1 — Exact value (for comparison).

Step 2 — Stratified estimator. With g(u)=eu−1e−1g(u)=\frac{e^{u}-1}{e-1}g(u)=e−1eu−1​, split [0,1][0,1][0,1] into kkk equal strata of width h=1/kh=1/kh=1/k. Draw mmm uniforms inside each stratum and combine:

Step 3 — Recommendation on intervals. The integrand ggg is smooth and increasing, so even a few strata help a lot. Going from k=2k=2k=2 to k=3k=3k=3 further reduces the within-stratum variation, so k=3k=3k=3 is recommended over k=2k=2k=2 (more strata = smaller within-stratum variance). Keep the same number of points per stratum (proportional allocation), with the total N=kmN=kmN=km as large as your budget allows (e.g. a few thousand).

Step 4 — Efficiency gain. Stratification removes the between-stratum part of the variance. The variance of the stratified estimator is only the average within-stratum variance, which for this smooth increasing function is much smaller than the total variance used by simple MC. The efficiency gain is

and for k=3k=3k=3 it is typically several-fold (the more strata, the larger the gain).

Algorithm.

  1. Choose kkk (recommend 3) and points per stratum mmm; width h=1/kh=1/kh=1/k.
  1. For each stratum draw mmm uniforms inside it and average ggg.
  1. Multiply each stratum average by hhh and sum.
  1. Compare its variance with simple MC to get the efficiency gain.

R code.

# Stratified vs simple MC for integral of (e^u - 1)/(e - 1) on [0,1]
set.seed(11)
g <- function(u) (exp(u) - 1) / (exp(1) - 1)

stratified <- function(k, m) {
  h <- 1 / k
  total <- 0
  for (j in 1:k) {
    V <- runif(m, (j-1)/k, j/k)
    total <- total + h * mean(g(V))
  }
  total
}

# Compare variability over many repetitions
reps <- 1000; m <- 1000; N <- 3 * m
mc_vals  <- replicate(reps, mean(g(runif(N))))      # simple MC, same total N
st_vals  <- replicate(reps, stratified(k = 3, m = m)) # stratified, k = 3

mean(st_vals); (exp(1) - 2)/(exp(1) - 1)            # ~ 0.41803
var(mc_vals) / var(st_vals)                         # efficiency gain (>1)

Expected output: the stratified mean is about 0.41803 (the exact value), and var(mc_vals)/var(st_vals) is clearly greater than 1, showing the efficiency gain of stratification with k=3k=3k=3.

Q19. Explain the test of randomness with an example. Approximate the test of randomness for large samples.

Solution.

Step 1 — What the test checks. A test of randomness checks whether a sequence of numbers behaves like an independent, patternless sequence, or whether it has trends/clustering. A common one is the runs test (runs above and below the median).

Step 2 — Building runs (small example). Replace each value by +++ if it is above the median and −-− if below. A run is a maximal block of identical signs. For the sequence

the runs are (++)(−−−)(+)(−)(++)(++)(---)(+)(-)(++)(++)(−−−)(+)(−)(++), so the number of runs is R=5R=5R=5, with n1=5n_1=5n1​=5 plus-signs and n2=4n_2=4n2​=4 minus-signs. Too few runs means clustering; too many means over-alternation. Either is evidence against randomness.

Step 3 — Large-sample (normal) approximation. For large n1n_1n1​ and n2n_2n2​, under the hypothesis of randomness the number of runs RRR is approximately normal with

The test statistic is

We reject randomness at the 5% level if ∣Z∣>1.96|Z|>1.96∣Z∣>1.96.

Algorithm.

  1. Compute the median of the sequence; label each value +++ (above) or −-− (below), dropping ties.
  1. Count the number of runs RRR and the counts n1,n2n_1,n_2n1​,n2​.
  1. Compute E[R]E[R]E[R] and Var(R)\text{Var}(R)Var(R) from the formulas.
  1. Compute Z=(R−E[R])/Var(R)Z=(R-E[R])/\sqrt{\text{Var}(R)}Z=(R−E[R])/Var(R)​.
  1. Reject randomness if ∣Z∣>1.96|Z|>1.96∣Z∣>1.96 (5% level).

R code.

# Runs test (above/below median) with the large-sample normal approximation
runs_test <- function(x) {
  s  <- sign(x - median(x))       # + / - relative to the median
  s  <- s[s != 0]                 # drop values exactly at the median
  n1 <- sum(s > 0); n2 <- sum(s < 0)
  R  <- 1 + sum(s[-1] != s[-length(s)])   # count runs

  ER  <- 2*n1*n2/(n1+n2) + 1
  VR  <- 2*n1*n2*(2*n1*n2 - n1 - n2) / ((n1+n2)^2 * (n1+n2-1))
  Z   <- (R - ER) / sqrt(VR)
  list(R = R, ER = ER, Z = Z, reject = abs(Z) > 1.96)
}

set.seed(3)
runs_test(runif(1000))   # random data: |Z| should be small, reject = FALSE

Expected output: for genuinely random data |Z| is small (well under 1.96) and reject = FALSE, meaning the test finds no evidence against randomness. Patterned data would give a large |Z| and reject = TRUE.

Group 4 — Inverse Transform & Discrete Generation (Q20–Q24)

Q20. Discuss the inverse transformation method to simulate data from a continuous distribution. How would you generate a random sample from an exponential distribution with mean λ\lambdaλ?

Solution.

Step 1 — The inverse transform principle. If XXX has a continuous CDF FFF, then the random variable U=F(X)U=F(X)U=F(X) is Uniform(0,1)(0,1)(0,1). Turning this around: if we draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) and set

then XXX has exactly the distribution FFF. So to simulate any continuous variable we only need its inverse CDF and a uniform generator.

Step 2 — Apply to the exponential with mean λ\lambdaλ. An exponential with mean λ\lambdaλ has rate 1/λ1/\lambda1/λ, density f(x)=1λe−x/λf(x)=\frac1\lambda e^{-x/\lambda}f(x)=λ1​e−x/λ and CDF

Step 3 — Invert the CDF. Set U=F(X)U=F(X)U=F(X) and solve for XXX:

Step 4 — Simplify. Since 1−U1-U1−U is also Uniform(0,1)(0,1)(0,1), we may replace 1−U1-U1−U by UUU:

This XXX is exponential with mean λ\lambdaλ.

Algorithm.

  1. Generate U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Set X=−λln⁡UX=-\lambda\ln UX=−λlnU.
  1. Repeat for as many values as needed.

R code.

# Exponential(mean = lambda) by inverse transform
lambda <- 2          # desired mean
n <- 10000
U <- runif(n)
X <- -lambda * log(U)        # inverse-CDF transform

mean(X)              # ~ 2 (the mean lambda)
var(X)               # ~ 4 (variance lambda^2)

Expected output: mean(X) is about 2 and var(X) about 4, matching an exponential with mean λ=2\lambda=2λ=2 (mean λ\lambdaλ, variance λ2\lambda^2λ2).

Q21. Discuss the inverse transformation method for a discrete distribution. Write the algorithm to draw a sample from Binomial(5,0.4)(5,0.4)(5,0.4), and the R code to simulate 1000 values (also via the Bernoulli distribution).

Solution.

Step 1 — Inverse transform for discrete variables. Suppose XXX takes values x0,x1,x2,…x_0,x_1,x_2,\dotsx0​,x1​,x2​,… with probabilities p0,p1,p2,…p_0,p_1,p_2,\dotsp0​,p1​,p2​,…. Build the cumulative probabilities Fk=p0+p1+⋯+pkF_k=p_0+p_1+\dots+p_kFk​=p0​+p1​+⋯+pk​. Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) and pick the smallest kkk with U≤FkU\le F_kU≤Fk​; output X=xkX=x_kX=xk​. Because the chance of landing in the slice (Fk−1,Fk](F_{k-1},F_k](Fk−1​,Fk​] equals its width pkp_kpk​, the output has the right distribution.

Step 2 — Binomial(5,0.4)(5,0.4)(5,0.4) probabilities. Using P(X=k)=(5k)(0.4)k(0.6)5−kP(X=k)=\binom{5}{k}(0.4)^k(0.6)^{5-k}P(X=k)=(k5​)(0.4)k(0.6)5−k:

The cumulative values are F0=0.07776, F1=0.33696, F2=0.68256, F3=0.91296, F4=0.98976, F5=1F_0=0.07776,\ F_1=0.33696,\ F_2=0.68256,\ F_3=0.91296,\ F_4=0.98976,\ F_5=1F0​=0.07776, F1​=0.33696, F2​=0.68256, F3​=0.91296, F4​=0.98976, F5​=1.

Step 3 — The Bernoulli route. A Binomial(5,0.4)(5,0.4)(5,0.4) is the sum of 5 independent Bernoulli(0.4)(0.4)(0.4) trials. Each trial is a success if a uniform is ≤0.4\le 0.4≤0.4. Counting the successes gives one binomial value. This is the easiest way to simulate it.

Algorithm (inverse transform).

  1. Precompute the cumulative probabilities F0,…,F5F_0,\dots,F_5F0​,…,F5​.
  1. Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Find the smallest kkk with U≤FkU\le F_kU≤Fk​; output X=kX=kX=k.
  1. Repeat 1000 times.

Algorithm (Bernoulli sum).

  1. For each of the 1000 samples: draw 5 uniforms.
  1. Count how many are ≤0.4\le 0.4≤0.4 (the successes).
  1. That count is one Binomial(5,0.4)(5,0.4)(5,0.4) value.

R code.

# ---- (A) Inverse-transform method ----
set.seed(8)
p  <- dbinom(0:5, size = 5, prob = 0.4)   # probabilities p0..p5
cum <- cumsum(p)                           # cumulative F0..F5

sim_inv <- function() {
  u <- runif(1)
  which(u <= cum)[1] - 1     # smallest k with u <= F_k; values are 0..5
}
x_inv <- replicate(1000, sim_inv())
table(x_inv) / 1000          # empirical probabilities ~ p0..p5

# ---- (B) Bernoulli-sum method ----
set.seed(8)
x_bern <- replicate(1000, sum(runif(5) <= 0.4))   # count successes in 5 trials
mean(x_bern)                 # ~ 2.0 (np = 5*0.4)

Expected output: both methods give samples whose relative frequencies match 0.078, 0.259, 0.346, 0.230, 0.077, 0.010 for k=0,…,5k=0,\dots,5k=0,…,5, and the sample mean is near 2.0 (np=2np=2np=2).

Q22. Write down the algorithm to generate an (n,p)(n,p)(n,p) (Binomial) random variate.

Solution.

Step 1 — Key fact. A Binomial(n,p)(n,p)(n,p) random variable counts the number of successes in nnn independent Bernoulli(p)(p)(p) trials:

Step 2 — Why it works. Each indicator IiI_iIi​ is 1 with probability P(Ui≤p)=pP(U_i\le p)=pP(Ui​≤p)=p, so it is Bernoulli(p)(p)(p). The sum of nnn independent Bernoulli(p)(p)(p) variables is Binomial(n,p)(n,p)(n,p).

Algorithm.

  1. Set the counter X=0X=0X=0.
  1. For i=1,…,ni=1,\dots,ni=1,…,n: draw Ui∼Uniform(0,1)U_i\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)Ui​∼Uniform(0,1); if Ui≤pU_i\le pUi​≤p, add 1 to XXX.
  1. Output XXX (one Binomial(n,p)(n,p)(n,p) value). Repeat for more values.

R code.

# Generate one (n, p) binomial variate from uniforms
rbinom_uniform <- function(n, p) sum(runif(n) <= p)

set.seed(21)
rbinom_uniform(10, 0.3)                       # one Binomial(10, 0.3) value
x <- replicate(10000, rbinom_uniform(10, 0.3))
mean(x); 10 * 0.3                             # ~ 3 (np)
var(x);  10 * 0.3 * 0.7                       # ~ 2.1 (np(1-p))

Expected output: the simulated mean is near 3 and the variance near 2.1, matching npnpnp and np(1−p)np(1-p)np(1−p) for Binomial(10,0.3)(10,0.3)(10,0.3).

Q23. Write down the algorithm to simulate a random variable from the Poisson distribution with parameter mmm.

Solution.

Step 1 — The idea (link to exponential gaps). A Poisson(m)(m)(m) count is the number of events of a rate-mmm Poisson process in one unit of time. The gaps between events are exponential with mean 1/m1/m1/m, and an exponential gap is −1mln⁡U-\frac1m\ln U−m1​lnU. So we keep adding exponential gaps until the total passes 1, and count how many fit.

Step 2 — The multiplicative form. Adding gaps −1mln⁡Ui-\frac1m\ln U_i−m1​lnUi​ until the sum exceeds 1 is the same as multiplying uniforms until the product drops below e−me^{-m}e−m. Formally, XXX is the largest kkk such that

This avoids logarithms and is the standard Knuth algorithm.

Step 3 — Why it works. Taking logs, ∑i=1k(−ln⁡Ui)≤m\sum_{i=1}^k(-\ln U_i)\le m∑i=1k​(−lnUi​)≤m describes the arrival times of a unit-rate process up to time mmm, whose count is Poisson(m)(m)(m).

Algorithm (Knuth).

  1. Set L=e−mL=e^{-m}L=e−m, k=0k=0k=0, P=1P=1P=1.
  1. Repeat: draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1); set P=P⋅UP=P\cdot UP=P⋅U and k=k+1k=k+1k=k+1.
  1. Stop when P<LP<LP<L.
  1. Output X=k−1X=k-1X=k−1.

R code.

# Poisson(m) by Knuth's multiplication-of-uniforms method
rpois_knuth <- function(m) {
  L <- exp(-m); k <- 0; P <- 1
  repeat {
    P <- P * runif(1)
    k <- k + 1
    if (P < L) break
  }
  k - 1
}

set.seed(23)
x <- replicate(10000, rpois_knuth(4))   # Poisson with m = 4
mean(x); var(x)                          # both ~ 4 (mean = variance = m)

Expected output: the simulated mean and variance are both close to 4, as expected for Poisson(m=4)(m=4)(m=4) where mean === variance =m=m=m.

Q24. Let XXX be a geometric variable with parameter ppp. Show that X=⌊ln⁡Uln⁡(1−p)⌋X=\left\lfloor \frac{\ln U}{\ln(1-p)} \right\rfloorX=⌊ln(1−p)lnU​⌋, where U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1). Write the algorithm to generate XXX.

Solution.

Step 1 — The geometric distribution. Take the version counting failures before the first success, X∈{0,1,2,… }X\in\{0,1,2,\dots\}X∈{0,1,2,…} with P(X=k)=(1−p)kpP(X=k)=(1-p)^k pP(X=k)=(1−p)kp. Its CDF is

Step 2 — Apply inverse transform. We want the smallest integer kkk with U≤F(k)=1−(1−p)k+1U\le F(k)=1-(1-p)^{k+1}U≤F(k)=1−(1−p)k+1, i.e.

Take natural logs (note ln⁡(1−p)<0\ln(1-p)<0ln(1−p)<0, so the inequality flips on dividing):

The smallest such integer is k=⌈ln⁡(1−U)ln⁡(1−p)⌉−1=⌊ln⁡(1−U)ln⁡(1−p)⌋k=\left\lceil \frac{\ln(1-U)}{\ln(1-p)}\right\rceil-1=\left\lfloor \frac{\ln(1-U)}{\ln(1-p)}\right\rfloork=⌈ln(1−p)ln(1−U)​⌉−1=⌊ln(1−p)ln(1−U)​⌋.

Step 3 — Simplify. Since 1−U1-U1−U is Uniform(0,1)(0,1)(0,1), replace it by UUU:

Algorithm.

  1. Generate U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Compute X=⌊ln⁡U/ln⁡(1−p)⌋X=\big\lfloor \ln U/\ln(1-p)\big\rfloorX=⌊lnU/ln(1−p)⌋.
  1. Output XXX (number of failures before the first success).

R code.

# Geometric (failures before first success) by inverse transform
rgeom_inv <- function(n, p) floor(log(runif(n)) / log(1 - p))

set.seed(24)
p <- 0.3
x <- rgeom_inv(10000, p)
mean(x); (1 - p)/p          # ~ 2.333 (theoretical mean of this geometric)

Expected output: the simulated mean is near 2.333, matching the theoretical mean (1−p)/p(1-p)/p(1−p)/p for p=0.3p=0.3p=0.3.

Group 5 — Generating Continuous Distributions (Q25–Q32)

Q25. Describe the Box–Muller method for generating normal random variables. How would you generate a sample from N(μ,σ2)N(\mu,\sigma^2)N(μ,σ2)?

Solution.

Step 1 — The idea. The Box–Muller method turns two independent uniforms into two independent standard normals using a polar (angle–radius) representation of the standard bivariate normal.

Step 2 — The transform. Draw U1,U2∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,U_2\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,U2​∼Uniform(0,1) and set

Then Z1,Z2Z_1,Z_2Z1​,Z2​ are independent N(0,1)N(0,1)N(0,1). Here R=−2ln⁡U1R=\sqrt{-2\ln U_1}R=−2lnU1​​ is the radius (its square is exponential, matching the chi-square of a 2D normal) and 2πU22\pi U_22πU2​ is a uniform angle.

Step 3 — Shift and scale to N(μ,σ2)N(\mu,\sigma^2)N(μ,σ2). If Z∼N(0,1)Z\sim N(0,1)Z∼N(0,1) then

Algorithm.

  1. Draw U1,U2∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,U_2\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,U2​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Set R=−2ln⁡U1R=\sqrt{-2\ln U_1}R=−2lnU1​​ and θ=2πU2\theta=2\pi U_2θ=2πU2​.
  1. Set Z1=Rcos⁡θZ_1=R\cos\thetaZ1​=Rcosθ, Z2=Rsin⁡θZ_2=R\sin\thetaZ2​=Rsinθ (two standard normals).
  1. Return X=μ+σZX=\mu+\sigma ZX=μ+σZ for each ZZZ.

R code.

# Box-Muller generator for N(mu, sigma^2)
box_muller <- function(n, mu = 0, sigma = 1) {
  m  <- ceiling(n/2)
  U1 <- runif(m); U2 <- runif(m)
  R  <- sqrt(-2 * log(U1))
  Z  <- c(R * cos(2*pi*U2), R * sin(2*pi*U2))[1:n]
  mu + sigma * Z
}

set.seed(25)
x <- box_muller(10000, mu = 5, sigma = 2)
mean(x); sd(x)         # ~ 5 and ~ 2

Expected output: mean(x) is about 5 and sd(x) about 2, confirming N(5,22)N(5,2^2)N(5,22).

Q26. How would you simulate a random sample from the density f(x)=4Γ(2) x e−2xf(x)=\frac{4}{\Gamma(2)}\,x\,e^{-2x}f(x)=Γ(2)4​xe−2x, x≥0x\ge 0x≥0?

Solution.

Step 1 — Recognise the distribution. Since Γ(2)=1\Gamma(2)=1Γ(2)=1, the density is

which is the Gamma distribution with shape α=2\alpha=2α=2 and rate β=2\beta=2β=2 (general form βαΓ(α)xα−1e−βx\frac{\beta^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta x}Γ(α)βα​xα−1e−βx with α=2,β=2\alpha=2,\beta=2α=2,β=2). (Note: the exponent must be −2x-2x−2x for fff to be a valid density; a “+2x+2x+2x” would not integrate to 1.)

Step 2 — Use the sum-of-exponentials fact. A Gamma with integer shape α=2\alpha=2α=2 and rate β=2\beta=2β=2 is the sum of 2 independent exponentials, each with rate 222 (mean 1/21/21/2). An exponential with rate 2 is generated by −12ln⁡U-\tfrac12\ln U−21​lnU. So

Step 3 — Check. The mean of this Gamma is α/β=2/2=1\alpha/\beta=2/2=1α/β=2/2=1, and the variance is α/β2=2/4=0.5\alpha/\beta^2=2/4=0.5α/β2=2/4=0.5.

Algorithm.

  1. Draw U1,U2∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,U_2\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,U2​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Set X=−12ln⁡(U1U2)X=-\tfrac12\ln(U_1U_2)X=−21​ln(U1​U2​).
  1. Repeat for as many values as needed.

R code.

# Sample from f(x) = 4 x e^{-2x}  =  Gamma(shape = 2, rate = 2)
set.seed(26)
n  <- 10000
U1 <- runif(n); U2 <- runif(n)
X  <- -0.5 * log(U1 * U2)     # sum of two rate-2 exponentials

mean(X); 2/2                 # ~ 1 (alpha/beta)
var(X);  2/2^2               # ~ 0.5 (alpha/beta^2)

Expected output: mean(X) is about 1 and var(X) about 0.5, matching Gamma(2,2)(2,2)(2,2).

Q27. Find the constant α\alphaα so that f(x)=α x(1−x)2f(x)=\alpha\,x(1-x)^2f(x)=αx(1−x)2, 0≤x≤10\le x\le 10≤x≤1, is a density, and describe how to simulate from it.

Solution.

Step 1 — Find α\alphaα. A density must integrate to 1:

Combine the fractions: 12−23+14=6−8+312=112\tfrac12-\tfrac23+\tfrac14=\tfrac{6-8+3}{12}=\tfrac{1}{12}21​−32​+41​=126−8+3​=121​. So α⋅112=1\alpha\cdot\tfrac{1}{12}=1α⋅121​=1, giving

(This is the Beta(2,3)(2,3)(2,3) density, since 12=1/B(2,3)12=1/B(2,3)12=1/B(2,3).)

Step 2 — Simulate by acceptance–rejection (uniform envelope). Use g(x)=1g(x)=1g(x)=1 on [0,1][0,1][0,1]. We need the maximum of f(x)=12x(1−x)2f(x)=12x(1-x)^2f(x)=12x(1−x)2. Differentiate:

Then f(13)=12⋅13⋅(23)2=169≈1.7778f(\tfrac13)=12\cdot\tfrac13\cdot(\tfrac23)^2=\tfrac{16}{9}\approx 1.7778f(31​)=12⋅31​⋅(32​)2=916​≈1.7778, so take the envelope constant c=169c=\tfrac{16}{9}c=916​.

Algorithm.

  1. Draw X∼Uniform(0,1)X\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)X∼Uniform(0,1) and U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Accept XXX if U≤f(X)c=12X(1−X)216/9U\le \dfrac{f(X)}{c}= \dfrac{12X(1-X)^2}{16/9}U≤cf(X)​=16/912X(1−X)2​.
  1. Otherwise reject and repeat.

R code.

# Acceptance-rejection sampling from f(x) = 12 x (1 - x)^2 on [0,1]
set.seed(27)
f <- function(x) 12 * x * (1 - x)^2
c <- 16/9                      # max of f at x = 1/3

sim_one <- function() {
  repeat {
    x <- runif(1); u <- runif(1)
    if (u <= f(x) / c) return(x)
  }
}
x <- replicate(10000, sim_one())
mean(x); 2/(2+3)               # ~ 0.4 (Beta(2,3) mean a/(a+b))

Expected output: the sample mean is about 0.4, the mean of Beta(2,3)(2,3)(2,3), confirming the simulation is correct.

Q28. Describe the acceptance–rejection method for a continuous distribution. Use it to generate standard normal variates, and give the R code.

Solution.

Step 1 — The method. Suppose we want to sample from a density fff but can easily sample from another density ggg (the envelope) for which f(x)≤c g(x)f(x)\le c\,g(x)f(x)≤cg(x) for all xxx, with constant c≥1c\ge 1c≥1. The recipe: draw Y∼gY\sim gY∼g and U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1); accept YYY if

Accepted values follow fff exactly, and the average number of tries per accepted value is ccc (so smaller ccc is more efficient).

Step 2 — Normal from a half-normal. Generate the magnitude ∣Z∣|Z|∣Z∣ first, then attach a random sign. The half-normal density is f(x)=2π e−x2/2f(x)=\sqrt{\tfrac{2}{\pi}}\,e^{-x^2/2}f(x)=π2​​e−x2/2, x≥0x\ge 0x≥0. Use the exponential envelope g(x)=e−xg(x)=e^{-x}g(x)=e−x (rate 1). The ratio is

This is largest at x=1x=1x=1, giving c=2eπ≈1.3155c=\sqrt{\tfrac{2e}{\pi}}\approx 1.3155c=π2e​​≈1.3155.

Step 3 — Simplify the test. With this ccc, the acceptance condition U≤f(Y)/(c g(Y))U\le f(Y)/(c\,g(Y))U≤f(Y)/(cg(Y)) reduces neatly to

Algorithm.

  1. Draw Y∼Exponential(1)Y\sim\text{Exponential}(1)Y∼Exponential(1) (i.e. Y=−ln⁡U1Y=-\ln U_1Y=−lnU1​) and U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Accept YYY if U≤e−(Y−1)2/2U\le e^{-(Y-1)^2/2}U≤e−(Y−1)2/2; else repeat.
  1. Attach a random sign: with probability 12\tfrac1221​ output +Y+Y+Y, else −Y-Y−Y. This is one N(0,1)N(0,1)N(0,1) value.

R code.

# Standard normal by acceptance-rejection with an exponential envelope
set.seed(28)
sim_normal <- function() {
  repeat {
    y <- -log(runif(1))            # Exponential(1)
    u <- runif(1)
    if (u <= exp(-(y - 1)^2 / 2)) {
      return(if (runif(1) < 0.5) y else -y)   # random sign
    }
  }
}
z <- replicate(10000, sim_normal())
mean(z); sd(z)        # ~ 0 and ~ 1

Expected output: mean(z) is near 0 and sd(z) near 1, so the accepted values follow the standard normal distribution.

Q29. Simulate a random sample from f(x)=2π x1/2e−xf(x)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,x^{1/2}e^{-x}f(x)=π​2​x1/2e−x, x≥0x\ge 0x≥0, using an exponential envelope YYY that has the same mean as XXX.

Solution.

Step 1 — Identify the target. This is the Gamma(α=32,β=1)(\alpha=\tfrac32,\beta=1)(α=23​,β=1) density, since 1Γ(3/2)=1π/2=2π\frac{1}{\Gamma(3/2)}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}/2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}Γ(3/2)1​=π​/21​=π​2​. Its mean is α/β=32\alpha/\beta=\tfrac32α/β=23​.

Step 2 — Match the envelope mean. Use an exponential envelope with the same mean 32\tfrac3223​, i.e. rate θ=23\theta=\tfrac23θ=32​:

Step 3 — Find the bound ccc. Form the ratio

Maximise by setting the derivative of 12ln⁡x−x3\tfrac12\ln x-\tfrac{x}{3}21​lnx−3x​ to zero: 12x−13=0⇒x=32\tfrac{1}{2x}-\tfrac13=0\Rightarrow x=\tfrac322x1​−31​=0⇒x=23​. Plugging back,

Step 4 — Acceptance rule. Accept YYY (drawn from the exponential) when U≤f(Y)/(c g(Y))U\le f(Y)/\big(c\,g(Y)\big)U≤f(Y)/(cg(Y)).

Algorithm.

  1. Draw Y∼Exponential(rate=23)Y\sim\text{Exponential}(\text{rate}=\tfrac23)Y∼Exponential(rate=32​), i.e. Y=−32ln⁡U1Y=-\tfrac32\ln U_1Y=−23​lnU1​.
  1. Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Accept YYY if U≤f(Y)c g(Y)U\le \dfrac{f(Y)}{c\,g(Y)}U≤cg(Y)f(Y)​; else repeat.

R code.

# Sample from Gamma(3/2, 1) using an exponential envelope with the same mean
set.seed(29)
f <- function(x) (2/sqrt(pi)) * sqrt(x) * exp(-x)     # target
g <- function(x) (2/3) * exp(-(2/3) * x)              # envelope, mean 3/2
c <- 3/sqrt(pi) * sqrt(3/2) * exp(-1/2)              # bound (~1.257)

sim_one <- function() {
  repeat {
    y <- -(3/2) * log(runif(1))    # Exponential(rate = 2/3)
    u <- runif(1)
    if (u <= f(y) / (c * g(y))) return(y)
  }
}
x <- replicate(10000, sim_one())
mean(x); 3/2          # ~ 1.5 (mean of Gamma(3/2,1))
var(x);  3/2          # ~ 1.5 (variance alpha/beta^2)

Expected output: the sample mean and variance are both near 1.5, matching Gamma(3/2,1)(3/2,1)(3/2,1).

Q30. Explain Johnk's method for generating Beta(a,b)(a,b)(a,b) random variables. Use R to draw a sample of size 100 from Beta(2,2.5)(2,2.5)(2,2.5).

Solution.

Step 1 — Johnk's theorem. Draw two independent uniforms U,V∼Uniform(0,1)U,V\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U,V∼Uniform(0,1) and form

If X+Y≤1X+Y\le 1X+Y≤1, then

If X+Y>1X+Y>1X+Y>1, reject the pair and try again. The accepted ratio has exactly the Beta(a,b)(a,b)(a,b) distribution.

Step 2 — Why it works (sketch). Conditioning on X+Y≤1X+Y\le1X+Y≤1, the pair (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y) has a density proportional to xa−1yb−1x^{a-1}y^{b-1}xa−1yb−1 on the triangle, and the ratio X/(X+Y)X/(X+Y)X/(X+Y) of such a Dirichlet-type pair is Beta(a,b)(a,b)(a,b).

Algorithm.

  1. Draw U,V∼Uniform(0,1)U,V\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U,V∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Set X=U1/aX=U^{1/a}X=U1/a, Y=V1/bY=V^{1/b}Y=V1/b.
  1. If X+Y≤1X+Y\le 1X+Y≤1, output Z=X/(X+Y)Z=X/(X+Y)Z=X/(X+Y); else repeat.

R code.

# Johnk's method for Beta(a, b)
johnk_beta <- function(a, b) {
  repeat {
    X <- runif(1)^(1/a)
    Y <- runif(1)^(1/b)
    if (X + Y <= 1) return(X / (X + Y))
  }
}

set.seed(30)
sample_beta <- replicate(100, johnk_beta(2, 2.5))   # 100 draws from Beta(2,2.5)
mean(sample_beta); 2/(2 + 2.5)                       # ~ 0.444 (a/(a+b))

Expected output: a vector of 100 values in (0,1)(0,1)(0,1) with mean near 0.444, the mean a/(a+b)a/(a+b)a/(a+b) of Beta(2,2.5)(2,2.5)(2,2.5).

Q31. Use the acceptance–rejection method with envelope g(x)=1g(x)=1g(x)=1 to simulate from f(x)=20x(1−x)3f(x)=20x(1-x)^3f(x)=20x(1−x)3, 0≤x≤10\le x\le 10≤x≤1. Compute the constant ccc.

Solution.

Step 1 — Check the density. This is the Beta(2,4)(2,4)(2,4) density, since 1B(2,4)=(2+4−1)!(2−1)!(4−1)!=5!1! 3!=1206=20\frac{1}{B(2,4)}=\frac{(2+4-1)!}{(2-1)!(4-1)!}=\frac{5!}{1!\,3!}=\frac{120}{6}=20B(2,4)1​=(2−1)!(4−1)!(2+4−1)!​=1!3!5!​=6120​=20. Good.

Step 2 — Find c=max⁡f/gc=\max f/gc=maxf/g. With g(x)=1g(x)=1g(x)=1 we need the maximum of f(x)=20x(1−x)3f(x)=20x(1-x)^3f(x)=20x(1−x)3. Differentiate:

Then

Step 3 — Acceptance rule. Draw X∼Uniform(0,1)X\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)X∼Uniform(0,1) and U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1); accept if U≤f(X)/cU\le f(X)/cU≤f(X)/c.

Algorithm.

  1. Draw X∼Uniform(0,1)X\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)X∼Uniform(0,1) and U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Accept XXX if U≤20X(1−X)3135/64U\le \dfrac{20X(1-X)^3}{135/64}U≤135/6420X(1−X)3​.
  1. Otherwise repeat.

R code.

# Acceptance-rejection from f(x) = 20 x (1 - x)^3 = Beta(2,4), envelope g(x)=1
set.seed(31)
f <- function(x) 20 * x * (1 - x)^3
c <- 135/64                    # max of f at x = 1/4 (~2.1094)

sim_one <- function() {
  repeat {
    x <- runif(1); u <- runif(1)
    if (u <= f(x) / c) return(x)
  }
}
x <- replicate(10000, sim_one())
mean(x); 2/(2 + 4)             # ~ 0.333 (Beta(2,4) mean)

Expected output: the sample mean is about 0.333, the mean of Beta(2,4)(2,4)(2,4), and on average about c ≈ 2.11 trials are needed per accepted value.

Q32. Describe the Marsaglia polar method and compare it with Box–Muller. Use R to draw a sample of size 100 from N(3,4)N(3,4)N(3,4).

Solution.

Step 1 — The Marsaglia polar method. Like Box–Muller it produces two standard normals at a time, but it avoids the (slow) sine and cosine by using rejection inside the unit circle:

  1. Draw V1,V2∼Uniform(−1,1)V_1,V_2\sim\text{Uniform}(-1,1)V1​,V2​∼Uniform(−1,1) and set S=V12+V22S=V_1^2+V_2^2S=V12​+V22​.
  1. If S≥1S\ge 1S≥1 or S=0S=0S=0, reject and redraw.
  1. Otherwise set f=−2ln⁡SSf=\sqrt{\dfrac{-2\ln S}{S}}f=S−2lnS​​ and output

which are independent N(0,1)N(0,1)N(0,1).

Step 2 — Comparison with Box–Muller. Both use two uniforms to make two normals. Box–Muller uses sin⁡\sinsin and cos⁡\coscos (trigonometric, slightly slower), and uses every pair. Marsaglia polar uses no trig functions (often faster and numerically more stable) but rejects the pairs falling outside the unit circle (acceptance probability π/4≈0.785\pi/4\approx 0.785π/4≈0.785), so it wastes some uniforms.

Step 3 — Scale to N(3,4)N(3,4)N(3,4). Here μ=3\mu=3μ=3 and σ2=4\sigma^2=4σ2=4, so σ=2\sigma=2σ=2; output X=3+2ZX=3+2ZX=3+2Z.

Algorithm.

  1. Generate a standard normal ZZZ by the polar method above.
  1. Return X=μ+σZ=3+2ZX=\mu+\sigma Z = 3 + 2ZX=μ+σZ=3+2Z.
  1. Repeat until 100 values are collected.

R code.

# Marsaglia polar method, then scale to N(3, 4)
set.seed(32)
polar_normal <- function() {
  repeat {
    v1 <- runif(1, -1, 1); v2 <- runif(1, -1, 1)
    s  <- v1^2 + v2^2
    if (s < 1 && s > 0) {
      f <- sqrt(-2 * log(s) / s)
      return(v1 * f)            # one standard normal (v2*f is another)
    }
  }
}

x <- replicate(100, 3 + 2 * polar_normal())   # N(mu = 3, sigma^2 = 4)
mean(x); sd(x)        # ~ 3 and ~ 2

Expected output: a sample of 100 values with mean near 3 and standard deviation near 2, matching N(3,4)N(3,4)N(3,4).

Group 6 — Special Distributions & Stochastic Processes (Q34–Q43)

(Note: your exam sheet has no Q33 — the numbering jumps from 32 to 34.)

Q34. A discrete random variable XXX has P(X=0,1,2,3,4)=0.25,0.30,0.20,0.15,0.10P(X=0,1,2,3,4)=0.25,0.30,0.20,0.15,0.10P(X=0,1,2,3,4)=0.25,0.30,0.20,0.15,0.10. Describe an efficient method to simulate XXX.

Solution.

Step 1 — Cumulative probabilities. Build the running totals:

Step 2 — Inverse-transform (table-lookup) method. Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1) and return the first value kkk whose cumulative probability is ≥U\ge U≥U. Each value kkk is returned with probability equal to the width of its slice, which is exactly pkp_kpk​.

Step 3 — Making it efficient. Two simple speed-ups: (i) order the values by decreasing probability so the most likely outcomes are checked first (fewer comparisons on average); (ii) for very large simulations, use the alias method, which returns a draw in constant time after an O(k)O(k)O(k) setup. For only 5 outcomes the ordered table lookup is already fast.

Algorithm (ordered table lookup).

  1. Sort outcomes by decreasing probability: 1(0.30),0(0.25),2(0.20),3(0.15),4(0.10)1(0.30),0(0.25),2(0.20),3(0.15),4(0.10)1(0.30),0(0.25),2(0.20),3(0.15),4(0.10).
  1. Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Walk through the sorted cumulative list and return the first outcome whose cumulative total ≥U\ge U≥U.

R code.

# Simulate a discrete variable by inverse transform (table lookup)
set.seed(34)
vals <- 0:4
prob <- c(0.25, 0.30, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10)
cum  <- cumsum(prob)

sim_one <- function() vals[which(runif(1) <= cum)[1]]
x <- replicate(100000, sim_one())
round(table(x) / length(x), 3)   # ~ 0.25 0.30 0.20 0.15 0.10

# (R's built-in equivalent: sample(vals, 100000, replace = TRUE, prob = prob))

Expected output: the relative frequencies are close to 0.25, 0.30, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10, matching the target distribution.

Q35. A continuous variable has density f(x)=x−22f(x)=\frac{x-2}{2}f(x)=2x−2​ for 2≤x≤32\le x\le 32≤x≤3 and f(x)=1−x6f(x)=1-\frac{x}{6}f(x)=1−6x​ for 3≤x≤63\le x\le 63≤x≤6. Estimate its mean and variance from 10,000 simulations.

Solution.

Step 1 — Build the CDF. Integrate each piece.

  • For 2≤x≤32\le x\le 32≤x≤3: F(x)=∫2xt−22 dt=(x−2)24F(x)=\int_2^x\frac{t-2}{2}\,dt=\frac{(x-2)^2}{4}F(x)=∫2x​2t−2​dt=4(x−2)2​, so F(3)=14F(3)=\tfrac14F(3)=41​.
  • For 3≤x≤63\le x\le 63≤x≤6: F(x)=14+∫3x ⁣(1−t6)dt=x−x212−2F(x)=\tfrac14+\int_3^x\!\big(1-\tfrac{t}{6}\big)dt = x-\frac{x^2}{12}-2F(x)=41​+∫3x​(1−6t​)dt=x−12x2​−2, with F(6)=1F(6)=1F(6)=1.

Step 2 — Invert (inverse-transform sampling). Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1):

(The first comes from solving (x−2)24=U\frac{(x-2)^2}{4}=U4(x−2)2​=U; the second from solving x−x212−2=Ux-\frac{x^2}{12}-2=Ux−12x2​−2=U and keeping the root in [3,6][3,6][3,6].)

Step 3 — Theory check. The exact mean is E[X]=23+3=113≈3.667E[X]=\tfrac23+3=\tfrac{11}{3}\approx 3.667E[X]=32​+3=311​≈3.667 and the exact variance is about 0.7220.7220.722; the simulation should land near these.

Algorithm.

  1. Draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. If U≤14U\le \tfrac14U≤41​, set X=2+2UX=2+2\sqrt UX=2+2U​; else set X=6−12(1−U)X=6-\sqrt{12(1-U)}X=6−12(1−U)​.
  1. Repeat 10,000 times; report the sample mean and variance.

R code.

# Inverse-transform sampling for the piecewise (triangular) density
set.seed(35)
n <- 10000
U <- runif(n)
X <- ifelse(U <= 0.25, 2 + 2*sqrt(U), 6 - sqrt(12*(1 - U)))

mean(X)   # ~ 3.667
var(X)    # ~ 0.722

Expected output: mean(X) is about 3.667 and var(X) about 0.72, matching the exact values.

Q36. Write R code to simulate 10,000 values from the double-exponential density f(x)=e2xf(x)=e^{2x}f(x)=e2x for x<0x<0x<0 and f(x)=e−2xf(x)=e^{-2x}f(x)=e−2x for x≥0x\ge0x≥0. Estimate the mean and variance.

Solution.

Step 1 — Recognise the distribution. For both branches f(x)=e−2∣x∣f(x)=e^{-2|x|}f(x)=e−2∣x∣, which is the Laplace (double-exponential) density with rate 222. It is symmetric about 0, so the mean is 0. Its variance is 2/22=0.52/2^2=0.52/22=0.5.

Step 2 — Simulation idea. Generate the magnitude as an exponential with rate 2 (i.e. −12ln⁡U-\tfrac12\ln U−21​lnU) and attach a random ±\pm± sign.

Algorithm.

  1. Draw U1∼Uniform(0,1)U_1\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​∼Uniform(0,1) and set magnitude M=−12ln⁡U1M=-\tfrac12\ln U_1M=−21​lnU1​.
  1. Draw a sign ±1\pm 1±1 with equal probability.
  1. Output X=±MX=\pm MX=±M. Repeat 10,000 times.

R code.

# Simulate 10,000 values from f(x) = e^{-2|x|}  (Laplace, rate 2)
set.seed(36)
n <- 10000
M <- -0.5 * log(runif(n))          # Exponential(rate 2) magnitude
sign <- sample(c(-1, 1), n, replace = TRUE)
X <- sign * M

mean(X)   # ~ 0
var(X)    # ~ 0.5

Expected output: mean(X) is near 0 and var(X) near 0.5, matching the Laplace(rate=2)(rate=2)(rate=2) distribution.

Q37. How would you generate a sample from a multivariate normal distribution using uniform random numbers?

Solution.

Step 1 — Target. We want X∼Nd(μ,Σ)\mathbf{X}\sim N_d(\boldsymbol\mu,\Sigma)X∼Nd​(μ,Σ), a ddd-dimensional normal with mean vector μ\boldsymbol\muμ and covariance Σ\SigmaΣ.

Step 2 — Independent standard normals from uniforms. First make ddd independent N(0,1)N(0,1)N(0,1) values Z=(Z1,…,Zd)\mathbf{Z}=(Z_1,\dots,Z_d)Z=(Z1​,…,Zd​) from uniforms using Box–Muller.

Step 3 — Impose the covariance by Cholesky. Factor Σ=LL⊤\Sigma = L L^{\top}Σ=LL⊤ where LLL is lower-triangular (Cholesky). Then

has mean μ\boldsymbol\muμ and covariance LL⊤=ΣL L^{\top}=\SigmaLL⊤=Σ, as required.

Algorithm.

  1. Compute LLL, the Cholesky factor of Σ\SigmaΣ.
  1. Generate Z\mathbf{Z}Z = ddd independent standard normals (from uniforms via Box–Muller).
  1. Output X=μ+LZ\mathbf{X}=\boldsymbol\mu+L\mathbf{Z}X=μ+LZ. Repeat for each sample.

R code.

# Multivariate normal from uniforms (Box-Muller + Cholesky)
set.seed(37)
mu    <- c(1, 2)
Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.8, 0.8, 2), 2, 2)
L     <- t(chol(Sigma))             # lower-triangular Cholesky factor
n     <- 10000; d <- length(mu)

# Standard normals via Box-Muller
U1 <- runif(n*d); U2 <- runif(n*d)
Z  <- matrix(sqrt(-2*log(U1)) * cos(2*pi*U2), nrow = d)

X <- mu + L %*% Z                   # each column is one draw
rowMeans(X)                         # ~ c(1, 2)
cov(t(X))                           # ~ Sigma

Expected output: the column means are near c(1, 2) and the sample covariance is near Sigma, confirming the multivariate normal sample.

Q38. Find the constant aaa so that f(x)=a x3e−x/2f(x)=a\,x^3 e^{-x/2}f(x)=ax3e−x/2, x≥0x\ge0x≥0, is a density. Give two algorithms to simulate from it and use one to draw 100,000 values.

Solution.

Step 1 — Recognise and find aaa. Comparing with the Gamma density βαΓ(α)xα−1e−βx\frac{\beta^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta x}Γ(α)βα​xα−1e−βx, we have α−1=3⇒α=4\alpha-1=3\Rightarrow\alpha=4α−1=3⇒α=4 and β=12\beta=\tfrac12β=21​. So

This is Gamma(α=4,β=12)(\alpha=4,\beta=\tfrac12)(α=4,β=21​) with mean α/β=8\alpha/\beta=8α/β=8 and variance α/β2=16\alpha/\beta^2=16α/β2=16.

Step 2 — Algorithm 1 (sum of exponentials). Since the shape α=4\alpha=4α=4 is an integer, the variable is the sum of 4 independent exponentials each with rate 12\tfrac1221​ (mean 2). An exponential with rate 12\tfrac1221​ is −2ln⁡U-2\ln U−2lnU, so

Step 3 — Algorithm 2 (acceptance–rejection). Use an exponential envelope with the same mean 8 (rate 18\tfrac1881​), find the bounding constant c=max⁡f/gc=\max f/gc=maxf/g, and accept/reject. This works even when the shape is not an integer.

Algorithm (Algorithm 1, used below).

  1. Draw U1,U2,U3,U4∼Uniform(0,1)U_1,U_2,U_3,U_4\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U1​,U2​,U3​,U4​∼Uniform(0,1).
  1. Set X=−2ln⁡(U1U2U3U4)X=-2\ln(U_1U_2U_3U_4)X=−2ln(U1​U2​U3​U4​).
  1. Repeat 100,000 times.

R code.

# Gamma(shape = 4, rate = 1/2) by summing four rate-1/2 exponentials
set.seed(38)
n <- 100000
U <- matrix(runif(4*n), nrow = 4)
X <- -2 * colSums(log(U))      # sum of 4 exponentials with mean 2

mean(X); 4/(1/2)              # ~ 8 (alpha/beta)
var(X);  4/(1/2)^2            # ~ 16 (alpha/beta^2)

Expected output: mean(X) is about 8 and var(X) about 16, matching Gamma(4,12)(4,\tfrac12)(4,21​).

Q39. Explain Gibbs sampling. Write the algorithm.

Solution.

Step 1 — The idea. Gibbs sampling is a Markov-chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method for drawing from a joint distribution π(x1,…,xd)\pi(x_1,\dots,x_d)π(x1​,…,xd​) when the full conditional distributions — each variable given all the others — are easy to sample. Instead of sampling the whole vector at once, we update one coordinate at a time from its full conditional.

Step 2 — Why it works. Cycling through the full conditionals defines a Markov chain whose stationary distribution is exactly the target joint π\piπ. After a burn-in period, the simulated vectors behave like draws from π\piπ.

Step 3 — Example (bivariate normal). For (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y) standard bivariate normal with correlation ρ\rhoρ, the full conditionals are X∣Y=y∼N(ρy, 1−ρ2)X\mid Y=y\sim N(\rho y,\,1-\rho^2)X∣Y=y∼N(ρy,1−ρ2) and Y∣X=x∼N(ρx, 1−ρ2)Y\mid X=x\sim N(\rho x,\,1-\rho^2)Y∣X=x∼N(ρx,1−ρ2), which are trivial to sample.

Algorithm.

  1. Choose starting values x1(0),…,xd(0)x_1^{(0)},\dots,x_d^{(0)}x1(0)​,…,xd(0)​.
  1. For t=1,2,…,Tt=1,2,\dots,Tt=1,2,…,T and each coordinate j=1,…,dj=1,\dots,dj=1,…,d: draw
  1. Discard the first BBB iterations (burn-in); keep the rest as samples from π\piπ.

R code.

# Gibbs sampler for a standard bivariate normal with correlation rho
set.seed(39)
gibbs_binorm <- function(T, rho) {
  x <- y <- numeric(T)
  s <- sqrt(1 - rho^2)
  for (t in 2:T) {
    x[t] <- rnorm(1, rho * y[t-1], s)   # X | Y
    y[t] <- rnorm(1, rho * x[t],   s)   # Y | X
  }
  cbind(x, y)
}

out <- gibbs_binorm(10000, rho = 0.7)
out <- out[-(1:1000), ]              # drop burn-in
cor(out[,1], out[,2])               # ~ 0.7

Expected output: after burn-in, the sample correlation between the two columns is near 0.7, the target correlation.

Q40. Write an algorithm to simulate the first TTT time units of a Poisson process with rate λ\lambdaλ.

Solution.

Step 1 — Key fact. In a Poisson process with rate λ\lambdaλ, the gaps between consecutive events (interarrival times) are independent exponentials with mean 1/λ1/\lambda1/λ. An exponential gap is −1λln⁡U-\tfrac1\lambda\ln U−λ1​lnU.

Step 2 — Build the event times. Start at time 0 and keep adding exponential gaps. Record each running total as an event time, and stop as soon as the total exceeds TTT.

Algorithm.

  1. Set t=0t=0t=0 and an empty list of event times.
  1. Repeat: draw U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1); set t=t−1λln⁡Ut=t-\tfrac1\lambda\ln Ut=t−λ1​lnU.
  1. If t>Tt>Tt>T, stop; otherwise record ttt as an event time and continue.
  1. Output the recorded event times (and their count N(T)N(T)N(T)).

R code.

# Simulate a Poisson process of rate lambda on [0, T]
set.seed(40)
poisson_process <- function(lambda, T) {
  t <- 0; times <- numeric(0)
  repeat {
    t <- t - log(runif(1)) / lambda    # add an exponential gap
    if (t > T) break
    times <- c(times, t)
  }
  times
}

ev <- poisson_process(lambda = 3, T = 10)
length(ev); 3 * 10          # count ~ lambda*T = 30

Expected output: a sorted vector of event times in [0,10][0,10][0,10] whose count is near lambda*T = 30.

Q41. Buses arrive at a stadium according to a Poisson process at rate 5 per hour. Each bus carries a Uniform(20,40)(20,40)(20,40) number of fans. Simulate the number of fans that have arrived by time t=1t=1t=1 hour.

Solution.

Step 1 — Structure (compound Poisson). The total number of fans by time 1 is

where N∼Poisson(5×1)=Poisson(5)N\sim\text{Poisson}(5\times1)=\text{Poisson}(5)N∼Poisson(5×1)=Poisson(5) is the number of buses in one hour and each Yi∼Uniform(20,40)Y_i\sim\text{Uniform}(20,40)Yi​∼Uniform(20,40) is that bus's load.

Step 2 — Expected value (check). E[S]=E[N] E[Y]=5×30=150E[S]=E[N]\,E[Y]=5\times 30=150E[S]=E[N]E[Y]=5×30=150 fans on average.

Algorithm.

  1. Draw N∼Poisson(5)N\sim\text{Poisson}(5)N∼Poisson(5) (number of buses in the hour).
  1. Draw NNN loads Y1,…,YN∼Uniform(20,40)Y_1,\dots,Y_N\sim\text{Uniform}(20,40)Y1​,…,YN​∼Uniform(20,40).
  1. Output S=∑YiS=\sum Y_iS=∑Yi​ (set S=0S=0S=0 if N=0N=0N=0). Repeat to study the distribution.

R code.

# Compound Poisson: fans arriving by t = 1 hour
set.seed(41)
sim_fans <- function() {
  N <- rpois(1, lambda = 5)           # buses in one hour
  if (N == 0) return(0)
  sum(runif(N, 20, 40))               # total fans across buses
}

fans <- replicate(10000, sim_fans())
mean(fans)        # ~ 150 (5 buses * 30 fans on average)

Expected output: the average total is near 150 fans, matching E[N] E[Y]=5×30E[N]\,E[Y]=5\times 30E[N]E[Y]=5×30.

Q42. A pair of fair coins is tossed until both come up heads. Estimate the average number of tosses by simulation.

Solution.

Step 1 — Probability of success on one toss. Tossing the pair, both land heads with probability 12×12=14\tfrac12\times\tfrac12=\tfrac1421​×21​=41​.

Step 2 — Distribution of the count. The number of tosses until the first “double heads” is Geometric with success probability p=14p=\tfrac14p=41​, so its mean is 1/p=41/p=41/p=4.

Algorithm.

  1. Set a counter to 0.
  1. Repeat: toss two fair coins; add 1 to the counter.
  1. Stop when both are heads; record the counter.
  1. Repeat many times and average the recorded counts.

R code.

# Toss a pair of fair coins until both are heads; count tosses
set.seed(42)
sim_tosses <- function() {
  count <- 0
  repeat {
    count <- count + 1
    if (runif(1) < 0.5 && runif(1) < 0.5) return(count)   # both heads
  }
}

tosses <- replicate(100000, sim_tosses())
mean(tosses)      # ~ 4 (theoretical mean 1/p = 4)

Expected output: the average number of tosses is near 4, matching the theoretical mean 1/p=41/p=41/p=4.

Q43. Let N=min⁡{n: U1+U2+⋯+Un>1}N=\min\{n:\ U_1+U_2+\dots+U_n>1\}N=min{n: U1​+U2​+⋯+Un​>1} where Ui∼Uniform(0,1)U_i\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)Ui​∼Uniform(0,1). Estimate E[N]E[N]E[N] using 100, 1000, 10000, 100000100,\ 1000,\ 10000,\ 100000100, 1000, 10000, 100000 simulations. (The true value is eee.)

Solution.

Step 1 — The setup. Keep adding independent uniforms until the running sum first exceeds 1; NNN is the number of uniforms used.

Step 2 — The remarkable fact. It is a classic result that

So the average count of uniforms needed to pass 1 estimates Euler's number eee.

Step 3 — Effect of sample size. With more repetitions the estimate gets closer to eee: at 100 runs it may be off in the first decimal, but by 100,000 runs it is usually accurate to about two decimals.

Algorithm.

  1. For one trial: set sum =0=0=0, count =0=0=0; repeat (add U∼Uniform(0,1)U\sim\text{Uniform}(0,1)U∼Uniform(0,1), add 1 to count) until sum >1>1>1; record the count.
  1. Repeat for RRR trials and average the counts to estimate E[N]E[N]E[N].
  1. Do this for R=100,1000,10000,100000R=100,1000,10000,100000R=100,1000,10000,100000.

R code.

# Estimate E[N] where N = number of uniforms needed to exceed 1 (true value e)
set.seed(43)
sim_N <- function() {
  s <- 0; n <- 0
  while (s <= 1) { s <- s + runif(1); n <- n + 1 }
  n
}

for (R in c(100, 1000, 10000, 100000)) {
  est <- mean(replicate(R, sim_N()))
  cat("R =", R, "  estimate =", round(est, 4), "\n")
}
exp(1)            # true value ~ 2.71828

Expected output: the estimates approach 2.71828 (eee) as R grows — rough at R = 100, accurate to about two decimals at R = 100000.
